speaker: interviewee
text: Brother, the one what died in Chicago. We thought that we're going to get our fancy-- our name a little bit fancier. So we just add another L and that's-- that is it. Now I was-- my name is Henry Wellner. My Jewish name is Chaim. I changed my name because in United States they-- they couldn't spell the name right. Chiam, Cham, Chamso. So I said, let's-- let's have a right name. I was born in Poland in 1988-- 1921 in Dabrowa, Gornicza, in Poland. I'm a son of a--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just chatting now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not on yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No, no. He's getting a sound check.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I didn't want you to get-- Today is October 23, 1995. The survivor is Henry Wellner. The interviewer is Bonnie Slavin. This is Miami Beach, Florida, the United States of America. The language is English. This is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Bonnie Slavin, S-LA-V-I-N. Today is October 23, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Mr. Henry Wellner of Miami Beach, Florida, the United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry. My Jewish name is Chaim. Henry Wellner.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: W-E-L-L-N-E-R. I was born in Dabrowa, Gornicza, Poland, on the 8th of 8, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- now I'm 74. Next year I'm going to be 75, if I live that long.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm a survivor from a family of-- from a very lovely family of eight people. I have one more brother. He lives in Chicago, but he-- he was-- he was not in-- during the war, he was not in Germany. He was in Russia. I also had another brother. He was also during the war in Russia. And he died in 1957 in Chicago. The rest of my family-- they all perished in Auschwitz in 19-- I-- I imagine. I wasn't home that time. I was in camp, in concentration camp. [SIGHS] They were-- I think this was in 1943, by [GERMAN]. This means when all the people, all the Jews-- the Germans just chased out from-- from Dabrowa, from-- you know, from the-- from the place where the Jews lived in [? Zegema. ?] [? Zegema, ?] Dabrowski-- that was also like a county, like a county.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Wellner, describe what your life was like before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, before the war. Before the war-- when the war broke out, I was about 16-- 16 and 1/2 years or 17 years-old. I had a very fine home. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Yeah, I had a very beautiful youth, and the surrounding of a very lovely family. I had my parents, and my three sisters, and five brothers. They-- I'm-- I was born in a beautiful home, in a good home, full of love. My-- I was born also in a very, very cultural home. All of us were educated. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was introduced to reading books in a very early age. And matter of fact, I had one sister, the youngest one, the youngest from the sisters-- she was-- she was something unbelievable. She finished, like I would say like here, college. She was one of the best in the city. We lived-- I lived in a Polish city of 30,000 people. 10% of the population was-- 10% were Jewish, Jewish people. My father was a merchant. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was-- he had a business, a wholesale business of all kind of foods. We delivered to groceries, not only in our city but in the surroundings. We were very well off, very well off. And, well, till-- till the war, till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I finished seven-- seven years of public school. Also, I had two years of night-- night classes in economy classes. What else can I tell you? Well, I had a very lovely home till today. Before the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, oh yeah. Yeah, I experienced a lot, a lot of anti-Semitism. The Polish people are very, very-- till today-- are very much the biggest, I would say that-- nah, I wouldn't. You have-- you have the Ukrainian, you had the Lithuanian. Anyway, the eastern part of Poland-- eastern part of Europe was very, very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see my nose? My nose was-- got crooked because young Poles throw a big, big stone and they hit my nose and fall. This was in 19-- I think this was-- that happened in 1938. And by my head was swollen for weeks and weeks till later on I didn't want to do anything [INAUDIBLE]. And the nose remained till today. I also felt very, very big-- very great anti-Semitism in the schools. They always beat up the Jews. They always beat up the Jews-- the young Poles. After the war, when I came back to my hometown to find out if somebody is still alive, they-- the only-- the only question from the Poles was, you're still alive? You're still alive? So many Jews came back. So many Jews came back. And I knew. So I asked them, how many Jews? Six? Eight? You know. They still had-- in their mind, they still had the hate, till today. Till today they hate the Jews most-- I-- most than anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we felt-- sure, we felt-- for instance, in the wintertime, they used to throw snowballs. They put in piece of a-- piece of a [NON-ENGLISH]-- a piece of, let's say, coals or to-- for the balls, for the snowballs to be harder, and we should feel it more than otherwise. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Also, about three, four years before the war, a priest went into the same business what my father was. He came in with a lot of money and so and so, with-- and he wanted to be a competition. But somehow he couldn't compete with-- with my father's business, because the Pollocks didn't believe him so much like they believed my father. My father was a very honest man, a very honest man, a good-- he bought merchandise for hundreds of thousands of zlotys, or dollars, just with a shake-- shake of his hands. Till today, I believe in the same thing. [CRYING] Well, my mother-- my mother stayed home. Her all-- her whole life-- their whole life was the family, the children, who had a beautiful mother. And her biggest pride-- her biggest pride were the children. And one-- one sister was married. One sister was married. She had a boy. 1943, I think, the boychick must be eight years old. She didn't survive. Also, the boy, the son didn't survive either. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I had-- I also had-- my oldest brother was also married. He had also a young child, a boy. My second sister was also married. Somehow it didn't work out. My third-- my sister, the youngest, but older than I, she married in 1941. And I remember that time it was the-- that time I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember, they send me a piece of cake from the wedding. She didn't survive it. And my-- the sister that I'm-- what I'm most proud of, she was a highly educated young lady. She finished like college, I would say. Her Polish was so well that she got-- she got from the Polish language, she had a 4. That's means not a 5, but a 4. She didn't want to go and pick up the diploma. She didn't want it. She had all passed, like excellent. She didn't want it. And you will see her picture. Like I mentioned before, that was a cultural home. We all read. We had newspapers home, books, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the languages spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish and Polish. My sisters, between themself, they spoke Polish. But they also knew Jewish very well. We spoke Jewish. My father-- we were a shomer Shabbos. Like before the war, like, you know, 50, 60 years ago, there was no such a thing like not to be a shomer Shabbos. But he was-- he was more pragmatic. He understood that if the business-- first of all, he has to attend the business world. Otherwise there's not going to be no [NON-ENGLISH]. No [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a very-- he was a very wise man, smart man. He was very good in business, trustworthy, like I said. All of us worked-- later on, worked in the business. Because we had to see the customers to go to collect the money, because all the business was on credit. And twice a month-- twice a month was payday in Poland, the 15th and the 1st of the month. So we had to-- we had bicycles. You know, there was no such a thing, like here in the United States, you have a car, you have a truck. No, we had bicycles. I traveled-- we traveled-- I traveled by bicycle.

speaker: interviewee
text: We employed about 10 people, mostly-- mostly with the horse and you know-- horse and how you call it? Horse and buggy, yeah? OK. So most of-- yeah. And like I said, we were very well off. Everybody knew my name-- our name.

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister. I met here a person a couple years ago, and I ask her-- she ask me, what is my name. I told her. So she asked me, did you have a sister [? Topcha? ?] I said, yes. So it was-- was a good, good, good life. That's-- if not the war, if not Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear about Hitler before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I was-- I was young, you know. In my mind there was no such a thing like politics or Hitler. But the first time we found out about-- I found out about Hitler, when he sent-- when Hitler-- the Germans sent back Polish Jews what lived in Germany. They didn't have a German citizenship. They sent them back to a place [? Sbonszyn. ?] [? Sbonszyn ?] was on the-- on the border of Poland, of Poland and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was in 1938. That time we started the-- you know. You know, my father-- my father wanted to send me-- and this was this sister what I'm talking so much about-- to Israel, to Palestine. There was a time when, as a capitalist with $500, we-- we could go-- we could go to Palestine, to buy 500 [INAUDIBLE] of-- you know, of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well you know how it is, before the war especially. My father ask the rabbis. They used to come from-- the rabbis used to come to collect money from the yeshivas, from big yeshivas-- Lublin, especially Lublin. So he ask him what he thinks about the idea. This was in-- this was in 1939, I think. No, this was in the beginning of '39-- something like that. We could-- we could easily go to Israel. He said, [NON-ENGLISH], he said, why you want to send that your daughter to Palestine? He said-- he said [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, that's how they-- that's the reason I like so much the-- when you talk about rabbis, I-- you know. You never forget a thing like that. And from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], this was it. This was it. Otherwise, we would go before the war. We would go to Palestine and it would be different.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah, when the war broke out-- and then in September, so the 1st of September-- this was on a Friday. Our town was not far from the border, from the German border, Katowice, Silesia, that's a [POLISH]. They call it-- in Polish, they call it [POLISH]-- Silesia. That was not far. Must be about what, 20, 25 kilometers from us. They came in Friday. I mean, they came into Poland Friday. And I think that Sunday we had them-- Sunday, Monday we had them in Dabrowa, in my town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't know what to do. We started to run, like you see now on the pictures from-- you know, on television you see people running from one place to the  other. So all of us run. All of us run. On the way to a town I met my father. He was on a wagon. You know, he must have buy the wagon with the horses. This was on a Friday, on a Friday the same Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a week later, I think, Friday, must be around 4 or 5 o'clock. All of a sudden, German planes came and they started to bomb, to bomb us. Not far from the road there was forests, big forests. And I run with my father. I run with my father to the forest. Started to get in the forest. It's dark anyway. And it's September. The days are shorter. And we lost each other. But before he lost-- before-- when he saw me, he gave me a package. I didn't open the package. I didn't know what the package is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I look for my father. I couldn't find him. And I start walking with thousands and thousands and thousands of another people, closer to the Russian border. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I came in-- I came into a little town, Kovno. I came into a little town and I stayed there with a family. I had-- I met another two boys. We started to live like together. I had the money. I didn't tell-- I didn't tell them right away how much money I had. Later on, I found out. I opened-- I didn't tell them right away how much money I have. But we-- we lived together. When was-- when was the end of the-- the end of the war? I think the 17 of September. We stayed in this little town about week or maybe 10 days. And we wanted to continue to the Russian border, to Russia, Rowne, over there. This was not far from Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got up early and I started to march toward the border, to the Russian border. And I see from far away Polish-- the Polish army, under-- under the Russian bayonets. So they started to tell me, the war is over. The war is over. The war is over. So I-- the war's over.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time-- at that time I-- the Russian had-- had about 10,000 POWs-- Polish people, Polish soldiers. And I met over there a neighbor of ours with the name [? Winelieb. ?] Dr. [? Winelieb. ?] He studied in Italy before the war. Later on, I found out that all the 10,000 soldiers the Russian killed in-- what is the name? It's a famous name. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They killed them. And they-- they said that the Germans did it. No, the Russian did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had-- yeah, so from there, I went to-- by train, I think by train, I went to Lemberg. This was a big city. [? Galicia. ?] Very beautiful city, a Pole-- a Jewish-- I mean, a large percent of Jewish people lived in Lemberg, a fine-- a big city, a nice city, a historical city. And over there-- over there I met a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked at me like I would come back from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the other world. He said, what are you doing here? What are you doing here? They say-- at home, they think-- no, this was-- no, this was later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided-- this was later on. We decided to go from Lemberg to go to Romania. So by train, we went from Lemberg, Stanislavov, [? Kolomyja, ?] [? Sniatyn. ?] [? Sniatyn ?] was the border of the Polish-- Polish-- Polish-Romanian border. On the other side, there was the city of Cernovic. And we heard that the Polish army crossed the border-- the Polish army crossed the border to Romania, to Cernovic. I thought-- we thought maybe we're going to be lucky. We're-- and from there, we had a mind to go to Palestine. But in Sniatyn-- we saw Cernovic. Sniatyn-- excuse me.
